Name: LatencyAwareInferenceThroughAdaptiveLayerSkipping
Title: Latency-Aware Inference Through Adaptive Layer Skipping in Dynamic Environments
Short Hypothesis: In dynamic environments, the computational overhead of deep neural networks can be significantly reduced by adaptively skipping layers based on real-time activation geometry, while maintaining high accuracy and enforcing strict latency budgets.
Related Work: Several works have explored methods to reduce inference latency, such as KIS-S for GPU-aware Kubernetes autoscaling (Zhang et al., 2025), TIC-VLA for latency-aware VLA models (Huang et al., 2026), and DELTA for efficient long-context reasoning (Entezari Zarch et al., 2025). However, these methods often rely on heuristic or pre-training approaches and do not dynamically adapt to changing input distributions. Our proposal introduces a training-free, adaptive layer-skipping framework that leverages real-time activation geometry to enforce strict latency budgets while maintaining high accuracy.
Abstract: Dynamic environments pose significant challenges for deep neural network inference, where computational overhead must be minimized to meet strict latency requirements. We propose a training-free, latency-aware inference framework that adaptively skips layers based on real-time activation geometry. By computing the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states, we identify tokens that are robust to low-precision computation and dynamically skip layers to enforce strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground this approach using PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability. Empirical evaluations on single-GPU setups across adversarial benchmarks and natural domain shifts demonstrate that our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier while maintaining low inference latency.
Experiments: Evaluate the effectiveness of the adaptive layer-skipping framework on a variety of neural network architectures, including ResNet and Transformer models, under different input distributions.
Compare the proposed method against static layer-skipping and full-inference baselines on latency and accuracy metrics using synthetic and real-world datasets.
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different spectral diagnostics (e.g., covariance condition number, spectral decay rate) on inference performance and latency.
Test the robustness of the framework under adversarial attacks and distributional shifts, ensuring that the adaptive layer-skipping mechanism maintains high accuracy and low latency.
Measure the wall-clock latency and FLOPs reduction achieved by the adaptive layer-skipping framework across different hardware platforms (e.g., single-GPU, edge devices).
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of the adaptive layer-skipping framework may vary across different architectures and input distributions, requiring careful calibration of spectral diagnostics.
The computational overhead of real-time spectral diagnostics may introduce additional latency, which needs to be carefully managed to ensure strict latency budgets are met.
The framework's reliance on spectral diagnostics may not capture all forms of input distribution shifts, limiting its generalizability in highly dynamic environments.
The theoretical bounds linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability may not fully account for all sources of model uncertainty, requiring further validation.
